story.description: A clip of reptiles: sparrow pecking on sandy beach in close up
story.hook: Snakes can sense heat when light disappears
story.lead: That snake just froze. No sprint, no lunge—just a slow sidewinder slide. That’s why the sparrow pecked right beside it. Snakes don’t chase; they wait, because heat spots on their face sense warm-blooded prey. That little sparrow. It knows the score. So here’s the part nobody tells you: the snake isn’t hunting the bird—it’s hunting the gap between pecks. Clever huh.

story.hook: Horses show why the face memory matters.
story.lead: See that grazing trick. Most horses keep one eye on the horizon while munching. That's because their wide-set eyes give them near 360-degree vision.
